data.title: ValuEngine Weekly Market Summary And Commentary
data.description:
Weekly Market Recap - Week Ending April 02, 2026

U.S. equity markets continued their upward momentum over the past week, with broad-based gains across major sectors led by technology, industrials, and small-cap stocks. Growth-oriented ETFs, such as the NASDAQ 100 and Technology Select Sector, stood out with strong advances that reflect renewed investor confidence in innovation-driven segments. Meanwhile, financials, materials, and communication services also posted solid gains, signaling a constructive risk-on environment. The notable exception was the energy sector, which faced pressure amid declining oil prices. This highlights a divergence in sector performance as markets re-calibrate expectations around global demand and macroeconomic trends.

In the below tables, we use major ETFs as a proxy for some major indexes as well as each of the sector groups into which we divide the overall markets. Tracking these over time provides a more defined picture of the US markets than simply tracking major indexes. This is followed by notable individual stock movers over the past month, and finally, our full strategy outlook.

Strategy Note

After a disastrous March for all the major foreign and domestic indexed ETFs invested in broad market indexes, US investors bought the dip last week. Nevertheless, we expect that most investors will see that as a “dead cat bounce” until more clarity is brought to the global geopolitical situation. For ValuEngine, the best strategy we can promote is to stay the course in using data-driven tools for investment decisions, not fears of wars raging out of control while the US struggles to find an exit strategy.

The good news is that our models now consider just 52% of the stocks we follow to be overvalued, as compared with more than 65% six months ago. Stocks recently upgraded to 4 (Buy) include: natural resources provider Cadiz Inc. (CDZI); BioCryst Pharmaceuticals Inc., (BCRX), a leader in the use of crystallography and structure-based drug design; Gilead Sciences (GILD), a leader in HIV and other immunodeficiency treatments; and hardware supplier Fastenal (FAST). Although we do not currently recommend large-cap health care as shown by our 2 (Sell) rating on the State Street Health Care Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLV), there are a number of biotech stocks that are recommended now for consideration for potential purchase.

Two new ValuEngine downgrades are in two of the industries most affected by the increases in fuel prices and global fighting. Airline JetBlue (JBLU) has been downgraded to 2 (Sell), and logistics provider RXO Inc. (RXO) has also been downgraded to 2. These companies will be challenged to maintain positive earnings streams until fuel prices stabilize and shipping businesses return to something resembling normalcy.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Olin: Improving Margins By Factoring Cost Savings
data.description:
Investment Thesis

During the Investor Day of 2024, Olin Corporation (OLN) set a bold milestone of reaching roughly $250 million in cost savings by 2029. This comes as the firm tries to bring in capital and operational efficiencies to counter the weak demand for its EDC and related products due to low construction activities that we have been witnessing for almost a decade. Thus far, Olin is succeeding in bringing in these cost savings. For the full year of 2025, the company managed to reduce costs by $44 million, and in 2026, it estimates more than $100 million of annual cost savings. If this estimate gets hit, more margin growth is to be expected, which will enable the firm to finance debt repayments, continue to pay dividends, and put itself in a great position to navigate these trough seasons without risking liquidity. Analyzing its 2025 financials, Olin shows better growth and profitability metrics than its industry peers while trading at a massive discount. Making me keen to rate it a buy.

Momentum Factor

Over the past 3 years, Olin has been an underperformer compared to its peers and the broader market. Most of this underperformance can be attributed to having a lower market cap, high debt levels, and weak demand for PVC products, as the construction industry has been largely depressed in recent years. In this 3-year period, Olin lost about 45% of its value, while Westlake (WLK) and the S&P 500 gained roughly 11% and 67%, respectively.

The scatterplot below shows that in the last 3 years, most names in this industry got hit pretty bad in price action. But we have started to see a price recovery YTD, with only 3 names on the negative y-axis. Dow (DOW) and Westlake scored an A and an A- for momentum, as they returned roughly 77% and 62% YTD, respectively. Olin returned only 37% and received a B rating. Momentum has improved just recently on this name because YoY, Olin returned 23% while its sector returned 36%, but in the last 3 months, it returned 40.37% vs. the sector return of just 6.53%. What this tells me is that more investors are starting to turn bullish, and if management successfully executes on cost savings, more buyers are to be expected as current multiples haven't priced in these improving fundamentals.

Management has taken advantage of this undervaluation to repurchase some of Olin's shares. In 2022, they executed the largest repurchase at a north of $1.3 billion, followed by a $700 million buyback in 2023. Last year, the number of shares that were bought back were valued at $51 million. In aggregate, the firm managed to reduce share count to 113.86 million.

Financial Analysis

Top line performance for Olin and its peers has been in a decline since 2023, and the peak growth decline for Olin hit 25% during 2024 on a trailing basis, the worst growth decline vs. peers. During the second half of 2025, the growth rate has flipped positive while peers remain in the negative territory, showing improving product mix and demand recovery in other segments of Olin's business. Adding the massive cost savings expected to be realized this year to this growth recovery will be a significant catalyst for margin expansion going forward.

Looking at Olin's revenue by segments, we can see that vs. 3 years ago, the top line got hit pretty bad across most segments, but there's some stability and growth on a YoY basis. CAPV revenue was at $5.09 billion in 2022; that revenue slid to $3.63 billion in 2024 and has since recovered by 1.38% to $3.68 billion in 2025. Epoxy revenue declined from $2.7 billion in 2022 to $1.23 billion in 2024 and has since recovered by 11.38% to $1.37 billion in 2025. Looking at the Winchester segment, we can see that the commercial side continued to witness revenue declines while the military has been trending upwards sequentially. This is due to low-volume selling and inventory reduction from the commercial side and high demand from the military side. Even the margin side of Winchester got hit by high input costs from copper and brass prices; adding to this margin contraction was high power cost in the fourth quarter due to cold weather conditions.

FY2025 Income Statement

Full year revenue increased by 4% to $6.78 million, with the epoxy segment leading growth by 12%, followed by the Winchester segment at 2% and the CAPV segment at 1%. The recovery in the epoxy segment mainly came from anti-dumping policies that intend to reduce low-quality and low-priced Asian exports to the European and North American regions. Due to the factors I stated above, the cost of revenue increased 8% to $6.28 billion, which led to gross profits decreasing 32% to $501.5 million and landing gross margins at 7.40%. Operating expenses increased 13% to $496.2 million, with $32.9 million coming from one-time restructuring costs, resulting in operating income decreasing 98% to $5.3 million and a pretax loss of $161.1 million.

FY2025 Cash Flow Statement

Net loss increased 196% to $101.1 million, but operating cash flow was positive at $474.2 million. What contributed to the positive cash flow was D&A, which increased moderately at 1%, and a positive working capital of $157.9 million. Free cash flow decreased 20% to $227.2 million, offset by capital expenditures of $226.3 million, which increased 16% YoY. Guiding for 2026, management expects capital spending, D&A, and pension income to be lower than the 2025 levels. Interest and restructuring expenses are expected to be at the same level. Factoring in the expectation of a cash-free tax year, FCF margins should be more than the current levels going forward, which should translate to more buybacks, debt repayments, and continued dividend payouts.

FY2025 Balance Sheet

While cash reserves decreased 5% to $167.6 million, current debt decreased 15% to $109.7 million, and long-term debt was flat at $2.72 million. A significant amount of debt maturities will be settled in 2030, valued at $1.04 billion, but around $370 million will need to be settled in 2027. This does raise some concerns as to how the company will be able to increase its cash levels while having to also pay dividends and interest expenses. Something has to give, and my gut tells me, share repurchases. I do feel like the firm might be cornered to pause buybacks to create enough liquidity room for itself. The firm has purchased a lot of shares already, so I see this as a minor setback to shareholder return.

Quantitative Analysis

Growth Factor

Starting with the growth factor, Olin is one of the few names in its industry that shows a positive YoY revenue growth; its closest peers are in line with the trend line in the third quadrant, showing 3 years and YoY of revenue decline. The growth rating has been upgraded to a D-, while Dow is still with a rating of F and Westlake with a rating of D. Olin grew revenue by 4% YoY, in line with its sector growth, while Dow and Westlake shrank revenue by 7% and 8%, respectively.

Profitability Factor

Olin also shows better profitability metrics than its peers. Even though this chart shows that most industry peers have a higher EBIT and FCF margin (they are in the first quadrant), Dow and Westlake are not found there and instead have a negative EBIT margin. Olin still got a C+ rating for profitability, and this was largely bolstered by the FCF margin of 5.50% vs. the sector margin of 4.82%, but other than that, broader margins still massively trail its sector median.

On an annual basis, we can see that FCF margins have been contracting significantly, but unlike its peers, Olin's margins remain positive, and given the factors I stated above, I expect these margins to trend even higher in the second half of 2026.

Valuation Factor

Despite showing better growth and profitability metrics, Olin remains trading at a discount to its peers. Even though the valuation looks fair on an EBITDA multiple (9.55 vs. sector median of 10.91), price to cash flow is significantly discounted, with an upside potential of 68% (6.85 vs. sector median of 11.79). Seeing Olin positioned below the trendline in the scatterplot below is illustrative of this discount.

Risk

While writing this article, I was intending to rate Olin a strong buy, but the debt levels made me question whether a strong buy rating is deserved. I don't think it does; even though maturities have been pushed years away, interest expenses are really hurting FCF and net margins. The chart below shows that Olin has one of the lowest interest coverage ratios while also showing a higher debt-to-equity ratio amongst its industry peers. With the current debt level, Olin would have to flawlessly execute these restructuring efforts and make sure that costs don't balloon beyond interest coverage, or else it could be forced to take on even more debt. Given that the firm is still tracing well on its cost-saving goals, this does alleviate debt concerns a little bit.

Conclusion

Olin has recently improved its top line growth, and with the massive cost savings expected to be added this year, I estimate FCF margins will continue to be positive or even trend higher than current levels. This makes Olin one of the few firms in its industry with the operational excellence to continue issuing dividend payouts and repay debt while navigating the tough environment that has been persisting for years. Growth, momentum, and profitability metrics look promising, but the firm remains undervalued relative to its peers and sector, giving us upside potential of 68% if multiples were to re-rate higher. For this reason, I rate Olin a buy.

data.title: Western Union: 11% Yield Is A Buy Signal On Margin Strength
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Western Union (WU) is one of the stocks I've covered most frequently on Seeking Alpha in recent years, with its chronic undervaluation appearing somewhat detached from operational realities, in my view.

A month and a half on from what I would suggest were pretty reasonable Q4 results, the firm continues to trade at a significant discount, with the dividend yield approaching a very generous 11% as a result.

As I've highlighted previously, valuation multiples applied to the firm (such as an adjusted P/E of ~5x) would usually be reflective of a business heading for bankruptcy. While the remittance industry faces headwinds, Western Union's core operations remain robust. With the stock down around 15% since early February, I would argue that now is a good opportunity to buy the stock.

Q4 Margin Strength

I wasn't able to publish an earnings review for the firm's fourth quarter and full-year report released in late February, though it is worth perhaps offering some brief comments on those figures in order to contextualize the rest of my up-to-date analysis on the firm.

In my last article on Western Union, I said that "if margins are stable or improved for the 4Q25, my bullish thesis is basically intact". Therefore, it was very encouraging to see the 20% Q4 adjusted margin at the same level as in the previous quarter. The more striking comparison, however, is with the fourth quarter last year, when adjusted margins were only 17%. This would suggest that the firm's previously outlined cost-saving initiatives, such as making KYC processes more efficient, have been highly effective.

With the remittance industry (and particularly the agent model used by Western Union) in somewhat of a secular decline, it was not surprising to see revenues down by 5% in Q4. I had warned of "a revenue decline of somewhere between 2-5% in consideration of enforcement intensification in the U.S. market" in my previous article, though clearly the results ultimately released came in toward the heavier end of my prediction.

Overall, though, the Q4 results were basically in line with my expectations, and the most encouraging aspect for me was the continued strengthening of the firm's margins, which are essential given the ongoing pressure on revenues.

Migration Policies

As I've previously argued, migration policy around the world (and particularly in the United States) has a significant long-term impact on Western Union and the broader remittance sector, given that the industry traditionally relies on migrant workers sending back a portion of their earnings to families in their countries of origin. Western Union's management team is acutely aware of the potential long-term impacts of migration policy, with CFO Matthew Cagwin recently highlighting that "we've seen immigration to the U.S. [...] dropped by about two-thirds from pre-Trump to where we are now".

Immigration policy was the overwhelming focus of my previous article, where I discussed the indefinite 75-country U.S. immigrant visa ban, which is still in effect. It is important to note, though, that a legal challenge to this ban (CLINIC v. Rubio) is now making its way through the courts. On March 10th, the plaintiffs in the case filed a Motion for Partial Summary Judgment, which might result in the ban being lifted more rapidly, though it is difficult to estimate the likelihood of this ultimately taking place. If the ban were ultimately lifted, it would certainly reduce some of the long-term revenue pressure in the North America region, which relies on the base of remitters being replenished with new arrivals over time.

As I've previously stated, it's easy to focus exclusively on the United States when thinking about outgoing remittances, but the recently filed 10-K covering the 2025 financial year shows that only 35.7% of revenue is attributed to the North American region. It also showed an absolute increase of around 5.8%, from ~$934 million to ~$998 million, in revenue generated from the Europe and CIS region, highlighting the increasing importance of this region for the firm as U.S. revenue softens due to the current administration's policies.

An important reason why I continue to be bullish on Western Union is that, while the U.S. is a tough market for the firm at the moment, immigration policies in other nations are not necessarily as hostile to the firm's operations. One good example of this is the recent announcement from the Spanish government of a new wave of regularization for illegal immigrants already in the country. Given that this will include anybody who has lived in Spain since 31st July of last year, it will cover a very large number of people and is quite likely to represent a pull factor for further migration to the European Union. According to Eurostat, Spain saw 1,288,600 immigrants in 2024, representing a large addressable market for Western Union. These migrants will, in some cases, now be sending larger amounts as they are given legal status and emerge from the "shadow" economy in Spain.

Geopolitical Impacts

At the moment, most public companies are being impacted to some degree by the conflict in Iran, and Western Union is no exception.

Interestingly, with the Middle East being a key source for outbound money transfers, the Iran conflict in the short term has been relatively positive for Western Union. CFO Matthew Cagwin also commented on this, noting that "typically, when there is a conflict, we will see people wanting to get their money out of the conflict zone". This is particularly relevant for the UAE, which has been noted by the firm as an important outbound remittance source. I would suggest that this is likely to bolster the firm's Q1 results somewhat.

Longer term, I think it's important to put the context into perspective. One could draw a parallel with the Russia-Ukraine conflict. In that worst-case scenario where Western Union had to totally suspend its operations in Russia, the long-term impacts of that are not particularly visible today, and there is not really the prospect of Western Union suspending its operations in any Middle Eastern countries at the moment in the near future, as far as I can tell.

The other thing to note is that, unlike many businesses, the fact that Western Union is a relatively capital-light operation means that it is unlikely to be affected by any of the direct commodity price or shipping-related considerations in the same way as traditional retail or commodity businesses, for example. Western Union is driven by migrants sending money back home to their families, which is relatively resistant to geopolitical considerations such as we are seeing in Iran. The indirect effect of worsening economic conditions everywhere would likely impact remittance volumes, but this sort of broad observation can be applied to most public companies, I would suggest. Overall, then, I am not overly concerned about the conflict as it relates to Western Union.

Operational Considerations

While "macro" and broad sector considerations are important, we also got a sense of what's going on operationally for Western Union when CFO Matthew Cagwin spoke at the Wolfe Research FinTech Forum on March 10th.

One interesting point related to the acquisition of Intermex, where Cagwin gave a sense of the management's motivations behind that purchase. He commented that in spite of Intermex's "very antiquated digital platform" they had managed to achieve 6% penetration (versus in-person retail). I would argue that there is an opportunity for further margin improvement by making Intermex's operations more efficient, potentially cutting down on expensive retail locations and migrating that business over to Western Union's digital platform.

I also got a similar sense of this quest for margin improvement in Cagwin's comments on the firm's new stablecoin, Digital Asset Network. The firm's intention is to "pull a lot of the capital out of the marketplace through using a digital asset" and this may "save the commission expense because you didn't have to go through an agent to pay it out". If executed properly, this will be fantastic for the company, though I am not totally convinced by the strategy, as I will explore in the "Risks" section below.

Risks to Thesis

There are a couple of factors worth considering that might represent a threat to my bullish thesis on Western Union. The first category is broader considerations such as migration policy and geopolitical considerations, which we have already discussed in some detail.

The second risk that investors should pay close attention to is the risk of an aggressive move over to the firm's digital platform and Digital Asset Network cannibalizing the agent network, which represents an important competitive advantage for Western Union versus its competitors.

As stated above by Cagwin, part of the motivation for the Digital Asset Network is reducing the costs associated with the agent network. The treasury side to the network is also likely to reduce fees paid to agents and correspondent banks to ensure that there is liquidity available for remittance payouts at all times.

While this can be mostly seen as a positive cost-saving initiative, the big risk is that Western Union "bets the farm" on a successful move over to almost exclusively using its digital platform while losing physical agents (particularly independent ones) by not paying them enough. The company does need to ensure a balanced approach, in my view, and needs to recognize that other players in the digital remittance space, such as Remitly (RELY), already have effective (and "sticky") digital remittance offerings. Since the firm's massive agent network is a profitable component of its business, I would argue that it is important that it not be done away with too aggressively.

Valuation

While there are risks for Western Union, I do think the margin of safety offered by the extremely low earnings multiple does mitigate my concerns about these to some degree. As I have hinted at, it is rare to see a (non-commodity) business trading at a P/E ratio of 5x without major concerns about its balance sheet or some other exceptional situation. Western Union's low earnings multiple alongside relatively stable and predictable quarterly earnings does make it highly unusual.

Also notable is the fact that intraday price swings over the past couple of days have pushed the firm's dividend yield very close to 11%. I think this is a clear case of being paid to wait for the firm's price to re-rate as the market eventually recognizes that the extreme investor pessimism surrounding the firm doesn't really align with operational reality.

Q1 Earnings Preview

Before concluding, it is worth just highlighting that Western Union is due to announce its Q1 results on 23rd April.

I expect normalized earnings to come in at around the consensus estimate of $0.40. This compares with $0.41 for the first quarter last year and $0.42, $0.47, and $0.45 in Q2, Q3, and Q4, respectively.

I am expecting a slightly worse result than the same time last year because Q1 is the first quarter where we are likely to see some of the impacts of the 75-country ban (introduced Jan 21st), and it's fair to say that the enforcement activity has broadly intensified in the U.S. between 1Q25 and 1Q26. At the same time, I do not expect earnings to be terrible, partially because of the artificial boost in Middle East outflows resulting from the Iran conflict but also because of the long-term secular strength in the firm's Europe region.

Conclusion

While the agent-oriented remittance business faces headwinds, it also represents an important component of a globalized economy in which migrant workers are an essential ingredient. U.S. immigration policy has had a tangible impact on the firm's results, but at the same time, it remains robustly profitable, with 20% adjusted margins in the last quarter. The firm's 5x earnings multiple also represents a significant margin of safety for investors.

Overall, then, I maintain my buy rating and believe that Western Union's stock remains an attractive opportunity.

data.title: The Brink's Company: Potential To Be A High Earnings Compounder
data.description:
Investment summary

My previous investment thought on The Brink’s Company (BCO) was a buy rating because the growth visibility has gotten clearer with AMS/DRS ramping up nicely and the valuation was still reasonable. I reiterate my buy rating as the recurring-revenue mix is getting larger and is now meaningful enough to move the needle, while NATL adds a second growth leg that could broaden BCO’s service offering and lift margin potential if executed well. At around 11x forward PE, I do not think the stock reflects this setup, especially if BCO becomes a stronger earnings compounder over time.

The recurring-revenue thesis is ramping nicely

My core view on BCO was that AMS and DRS will become a bigger part of the business over time, and that should lift both growth quality and margins. Recall earlier, AMS and DRS were growing 16% organically and made up ~25% of total revenue on a trailing twelve months [TTM] basis. Those figures have accelerated to 22% organic growth, and the mix is now 28% of TTM revenue. The momentum is apparently not stopping. Management is guiding mid-to-high teens organic growth for AMS/DRS in 2026, with a mix moving to 30% to 32%. As the mix becomes larger, I don’t think the market can continue to discount this part of the business as "A small part of BCO." When a revenue stream moves from about one-quarter of the base toward one-third and is still growing at mid-teens organically, the contribution shifts from low single digits to mid-single digits.

While the traditional CVM was flat organically, and overall blended organic growth came in at mid-single-digit, if we assume traditional CVM stays flat and AMS/DRS becomes, say, 80% of the business, there is no reason to believe the headline revenue growth will not accelerate.

NATL

A new growth catalyst to point out is NCR Atleos [NATL]. BCO agreed to acquire NATL for $6.6 billion, made up of 13.3 million BCO shares, $2.2 billion in cash, and the assumption of $2.6 billion of NATL debt. Management's view on this acquisition is that it would broaden BCO’s service offering and better connect AMS, DRS, and traditional CVM. They are also targeting $200 million of annual run-rate cost synergies within three years of closing, with the deal expected to be at least 35% accretive to EPS and to close in 1Q27.

For context, NATL is a self-service banking and ATM infrastructure business. It is a leading ATMaaS and managed services provider, one of the largest independently owned and operated ATM networks globally, and also a leading provider of multi-vendor ATM software applications and middleware. Most importantly, this is not a start-up. NATL already has a large installed base, including owned and operated ATMs, and this is why I believe it fits well into the BCO ecosystem. BCO already had a strong position in the cash, service, and route sides of ATM operations through AMS. With NATL, BCO is moving further up the value chain, whereby it is getting closer to controlling more of the ATM ownership model end to end.

This is a really interesting point to note because NATL adds a real growth leg to BCO’s growth story. Here is what I expect to happen. For banks, BCO’s pitch will become stronger as it can now offer a more integrated solution that can lower the cost to serve and simplify ATM ownership through a single-vendor setup. As for retailers, I think the appeal is pretty straightforward. BCO should be able to streamline more of the cash and ATM workflow inside stores because BCO now controls more of the stack (software, hardware, monitoring, processing, cash forecasting, replenishment, and field service). That should improve routing and scheduling as well, since one platform can match all the retailer needs, it makes sense for retailers to just stay with BCO as it lowers complexity, has better uptime, and lessens the need to manage multiple vendors.

As such, this NATL catalyst will add on to the already strong fundamentals of BCO. If the deal closes and integration goes well, BCO could end up with more recurring and subscription-like revenue, a wider solution set, and more room for margin improvement than what I already thought.

Valuation

BCO’s overall FY2025 revenue growth and earnings were pretty much in line with my expectations. Yes, adj. earnings margin was a miss, but at the current valuation of 11x forward PE, the stock has turned cheap again. In my model, I now expect growth to accelerate to low-teen percentages in FY2028, assuming the NATL deal comes through. Margin-wise, I expect BCO margins to go even higher as BCO should achieve both its revenue and cost synergies with NATL. I would assume multiples will go up if BCO becomes a >20% earnings compounder, but even if I assume 11x NTM PE (the multiple today), the stock is still worth 55% more.

Risk

The main risk now is the NATL execution. If regulatory approval takes longer, integration is not well done, or the $200 million synergy target falls short, the market may turn a lot more negative on this deal. And this matters because my growth acceleration and margin expansion assumption relies partially on this deal, not just on AMS/DRS.

Conclusion

I reiterate my buy rating on BCO. The AMS/DRS side of the story continues to work. But NATL adds a new path for BCO to deepen customer relationships and potentially accelerate growth and margins beyond what I had initially thought. I see execution risk around the deal, but I am hopeful it will turn out well. At the current valuation, I believe the upside remains attractive.

data.title: Sidus Space: Compelling Transition Narrative, But The Risk-Reward Is Not
data.description:
Sidus Space (SIDU) is one of the space technology companies I have been following. Founded in 2012 under the name Craig Technologies Aerospace Solutions (CTAS), it was rebranded into SIDU and went public in 2021. The company focuses on satellite manufacturing, autonomous space operations, and space data solutions mostly for clients in defense, agriculture, and government sectors, generating its revenues through delivering those activities as a Space-as-a-Service (SPaaS) business.

Since going public in 2021, share performance has been heavily underwhelming and volatile. Today, the stock trades at around $3 per share, losing over 90% of its value, despite the signs of recovery over the past year, where the stock has seen over a 140% increase in share price. Most recently, following the Q4 2025 earnings release just a few days ago, it seems that shares have jumped over 45%, possibly due to improved operating performance, suggesting that there could be some renewed enthusiasm for the stock.

I initiated my coverage with a neutral rating. My 1-year price target of $3.14 per share projects a 1.6% upside YoY, suggesting that SIDU does not offer an attractive risk-reward yet at this point.

Financial Reviews

Overall, fundamentals appear relatively weak. Revenue, for instance, saw a YoY decline in Q4 2025. In Q4, SIDU delivered a revenue of $1.1 million, which brings the FY 2025 revenue to around $3.4 million, a -28% YoY decline. This was mainly due to the overall business transition into potentially higher-margin data services, as commented by the management in the earnings call:

While this reflects a decrease of about $1.3 million or 28%, the change aligns with our strategic shift away from legacy contract work toward higher-value commercial space-based and AI-driven solutions. This repositioning is intentional and expected to generate more sustainable recurring revenue in future periods.

Source: Q4 earnings call.

The lackluster picture also extends to profitability, with the FY net loss widening to -$29.5 million, which was driven primarily by the significantly widened gross loss margin, which was -168% in FY 2025. Looking at this, SIDU seems to have a long way to go to break even, though given the new focus towards recurring revenue, my view is that this strategic shift should, on paper, be positive for the profitability situation as well in the future.

For the time being, liquidity remains safe, especially after the equity raise financing of $53 million as per its 10K, which brings the total cash to $43.2 million after offsetting the around -$18 million operating cash flow (OCF) burn. However, the fact that SIDU remains a cash-burning company that still relies on outside financing to sustain its operations today with widening losses does not present a compelling picture. However, my simple math suggests that, with $18 million to $20 million of OCF burn a year, the current cash position could probably still sustain SIDU for the next 24 months, give or take, meaning that it may not need to raise capital until early 2028.

Catalyst

Overall, I believe SIDU should expect a moderate catalyst into 2026. The main catalyst, in my view, will potentially be driven primarily by the launch of new satellite products, LizzieSat 4 (LS4) and 5 (LS 5), which seem to be equipped with new features and capabilities that should further support the management’s transition objective, which is to unlock revenue diversification away from just satellite design and manufacturing services, as commented by the management:

Unlike others that may depend primarily on government contracts to finance and build their satellites, we made a deliberate decision to create a Sidus-owned platform, including the underlying intellectual property that can support commercial, civil space and defense customers on a single satellite. This dual-use multi-mission model creates diversified revenue streams, broadens customer opportunities and supports a more resilient business model in an increasingly dynamic geopolitical environment.

Source: Q4 earnings call.

On paper, the transition seems positive, since if SIDU could indeed expand into the space-based data-as-a-service segment, it will have a more predictable, recurring revenue generation that could unlock re-rating.

As I mentioned earlier, with LS 4 and LS 5, which the company claims to be software-defined systems, SIDU should have the right products to simply turn its satellites into a platform that delivers more value and produces multiple revenue streams. For instance, as commented by the management, with the new products, they now could process AI-driven data on board, allowing computation and data commercialization to be done in real-time, which should be a compelling value proposition:

So here are our key areas to watch over the next 12 to 18 months. LS-4 and LS-5 are in production as software-defined satellites with advanced onboard AI processing and Fortis VPX, enabling on-orbit data processing, autonomy and mission adaptability. The Fortis VPX platform is beginning customer deployment, marking a key step in commercializing ruggedized multi-domain compute solutions.

Source: Q4 earnings call.

Though in the end re-rating will depend on the execution, the fact that the satellites are already in advanced production phase and that SIDU still has over $40 million in cash may have also slightly alleviated some concern about liquidation risk, which usually disrupts many frontier tech companies.

Risk

Meanwhile, risk is also moderate, in my opinion. I think that considering SIDU’s loss-making and cash-burning situations today, the formula for re-rating is probably quite straightforward. Since the overall growth narrative seems to be quite compelling to the market, as marked by the recent jump in share price, the next step for SIDU is to demonstrate success in execution, as I mentioned in the catalyst section. The reason is quite simple. If SIDU could indeed generate space data-as-a-service recurring revenue, it should deliver higher margins that could help offset its loss-making situation and achieve future breakeven.

Therefore, though the liquidity situation remains secure as of today, the key risk remains execution. Say, if future LS 4 or LS 5 launches face delays or on-orbit malfunctions, resulting in stalling transition, then SIDU may not only potentially face deep correction, but also have to consider further dilutive capital raises, in my view.

Valuation / Pricing

My target price for SIDU is driven by the following assumptions for the bull vs bear scenarios of the FY 2026 target price model based on the consensus estimate:

  1. Bull scenario (70% probability) assumptions - I project successful launches of LS 4 and LS 5, with the products delivering recurring revenue as expected. This means SIDU will reach the high end of its revenue estimate. I applied a 25x P/S multiple, which suggests a re-rating from TTM P/S of 22x today, as the market revalues SIDU as a space data platform with a higher quality recurring revenue stream.

  2. Bear scenario (30% probability) assumptions - I project slight delays in LS 4 and LS 5 launches due to technical failures, resulting in SIDU missing the consensus revenue estimate by $1 million and more pressure on its liquidity. I applied a 22x P/S multiple, where it roughly is today, suggesting that the market does not yet consider SIDU’s transition into a data-focused and diverse SPaaS platform as successful.

Consolidating all the information above into my model, I arrived at an FY 2026 weighted target price of $3.1 per share, projecting about 1.6% upside. This means that I would rate the stock neutral. Even with a relatively aggressive forecast that favors the bull scenario with a 70-30 bull-bear probability, it seems that SIDU does not really present a decent risk-reward, in my opinion.

Conclusion

SIDU appears to be transitioning from project-focused into a potentially higher-margin true SPaaS player, driven by the launch of its two new satellite products that could unlock an additional data-based revenue stream to boost profitability. This should be a good re-rating story for SIDU, which has struggled with losses and cash burns. However, it all still depends on how well SIDU could execute its transition plans. My price target analysis further suggests that SIDU offers a low margin of error for investors, in that a potential revenue miss could possibly result in a significant correction. As such, as I weigh the outcome of the bull and bear scenarios with my 70-30 probability, the resulting forecasted upside of 1.6% suggests that SIDU does not present an attractive risk-reward opportunity for investors.

data.title: Northwest Natural Holding: Fundamentally Sound But Fully Priced With Downside Risks (Downgrade)
data.description:
About five months since the publication of my initial article, Northwest Natural Holding Company (NWN) has already delivered 13% returns, justifying my buy rating. But because it has already reached my TP, or target price, I decided to take a look at this stock again. Its fundamentals are still strong, while dividends remain stable. However, I’m starting to see some caution as it appears fully valued. Technicals are still generally upward, but the market is starting to shift to a cautious stance as buying volume decreases.

Northwest Natural Holding Company: It Has Remained Strong Recently

The natural gas utility market may not be as volatile as actual producers today. Yet, they are not totally shielded from inflationary headwinds and natural gas price swings despite more regulated rates. Even an established local utility distributor like Northwest Natural Holding Company is subject to potential risks, although there can be nuances. But with its strategic management of gas storage as well as its increasing coverage in Oregon and Washington, it helps it sustain growth and margins. This was shown in its most recent performance.

In Q4 2025, its operating revenue amounted to $394.2M, up by 6.2% YoY from $370.9M. This YoY increase was also stronger than in Q4 2024 versus Q4 2023 at only 4.2%, which shows its stronger performance in the past year. We may attribute this to its increased domestic capacity. Also, SiEnergy, which NWN acquired last year, is now part of its operations. This allowed NWN to expand to growth markets, particularly in Texas. This also means more customers served and higher base rates. If you look at its operating statistics, you can see a huge increase in total meters across all segments. Another potential driver was the sharp increase in natural gas prices, which also allowed NWN to raise customer base rates.

Meanwhile, its operating expenses decreased, which I found impressive despite its larger operating capacity and stubborn inflation. This may be attributed to its ample gas reserves. Although this is not exactly a traditional midstream company, it still has underground gas storage to keep up with seasonal demand spikes. So even if natural gas became more expensive, it was able to suffice customers’ needs while managing its expenses. As a result, its operating margin increased to 28.3% versus 22.8% YoY.

Business Model: The Pros and the Cons

The business model of this stock may have some pros and cons.

On the negative side, growth prospects and pricing flexibility may be limited due to base rate regulations. So, it cannot simply increase or decrease the commission rates it generates. Of course, they may increase based on the demand and supply dynamics. But overall, I think it's a bit boring.

On the other hand, boring means stable most of the time. Base rates are not easily affected by natural gas prices. This is quite crucial today if we look at the sharp price decrease in the past month. So even if natural gas becomes cheaper, its base rates won't automatically drop.

Core Strengths and Drivers

One thing I like about NWN is its prudent gas distribution and reserve management. NWN may not be a midstream company in the traditional sense, but it plays a crucial role in ensuring that gas utility is distributed to customers. This is supported by its continued expansion, considering its acquisition of SiEnergy and the increasing lengths of its distribution pipelines. This allows it to cover a larger portion of Oregon and Texas. More importantly, it ensures adequate storage of natural gas. So, it maintains enough supply, especially during seasonal demand spikes. This also helps manage its gas costs when the natural gas price skyrockets, just like what happened in its Q4 2025 performance.

SiEnergy may also become an important driver as it gives NWN exposure to Texas. Texas is one of the states with the strongest economy, so businesses and households are more capable of paying higher rates if NWN is allowed to raise them to cope with the rising prices and energy market volatility. One aspect to consider is the robust business climate in the state. As you can see, TX had the highest net change in Fortune 500 companies in recent years. This means more job opportunities for the population. Also, larger companies may need more energy utility in their day-to-day operations, so NWN may see more opportunities here.

In addition, TX had the highest electricity consumption of 492.8 TWh in 2023. With that, it is also not surprising that it had the highest natural gas consumption. And because natural gas is the primary electricity source, the positive spillovers into NWN must be considered. This may be spurred by the data center expansion, which may require more electricity. In fact, data center capacity demand is expected to increase by 3.5x by 2025-2030. In turn, electricity consumption of data centers in the US may reach 606 TWh. So, natural gas demand may increase, and more volume may be distributed. This may lead to the increase in base rates, proving that its continued expansion there makes sense.

Liquidity

Concerning its liquidity, I have noticed its high capex, which could be attributed to its continued expansion. The challenge is that operating cash flow could not cover it. It also spent $338M in acquisition-related expenditures. With that, it had to issue $760M in debt to cover these and sustain dividends and repayments on maturing borrowings.

Meanwhile, its Balance Sheet remains decent with $37M in cash and cash equivalents. Borrowings are high at $2.6B, but 87% of the total will not mature within the year. My only concern is its slightly high Net Debt/EBITDA of 5.4x. Theoretically, this means that that business needs at least 5.4 years to repay the whole amount even if it does not have to. This also means that it is highly leveraged. The consolation is that 74% of the total has a maturity of more than five years. So, it still has enough buffer to sustain its capacity and dividends.

Valuation

To reassess its valuation, I updated its price ratios. Its P/E shows that the stock trades at 19.39x its EPS versus the five-year average of 17.90x. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP, or target price, will be $49.52. Now, one may argue that a P/E of less than 20x can be considered cheap. That’s correct somehow. However, it is important to note that it is already trading at a slightly elevated level than in previous years. In addition, the EPS is high relative to its EPS growth of 8% in five years. With that, its PEG is quite high at 2.4x, which can suggest potential overvaluation.

The same applies to its P/B, which leads to a TP of $51.09. This justifies the price increase from my previous coverage. However, it also shows that the stock is already fully priced with some downside risks. Lastly, P/S leads to a TP of $46.12.

Technicals

NWN’s technicals are still bullish as the uptrend stays above the 50-day and 200-day SMAs. This also shows that the current momentum is still stronger than the average in 50 and 200 days. The 50-day SMA is also still increasing, which means that market sentiments are still generally positive.

However, its MACD and RSI warrant some caution. The MACD line has recently decreased, which shows decreasing buying volume. We can also see early bearish divergence signals. You can compare the increasing price highs versus the decreasing MACD highs on February 25 and April 2. The histogram is still within positive territory, which implies that buying volume still outpaces selling volume. But the recent decrease shows that fewer buyers were entering as the stock became more expensive. Lastly, RSI shows that the stock is already overbought, so downside risks due to profit-taking must be considered.

Conclusion

Northwest Natural Holding Company is still a robust company with sustained growth and margins amid its expansion. However, investors may have to be a bit careful amid the recent capex and borrowing surge following its expansion, although liquidity remains decent. Its valuation also tells us that the stock is already fully valued. Technicals adhere to it as early bearish divergence calls in line with overbuying may have some downside risks. So, I am downgrading this stock from buy to hold for now.

data.title: Phreesia: Market Delivers A Resounding Vote Of No Confidence (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Last year, when I reiterated the case for buying Phreesia (PHR), I set my next purchase point conditional on a stock recovery above its 200-day moving average (DMA). That rebound never happened, and the stock delivered a fresh reminder of the importance of technical signals between earnings reports. The market was unhappy with last year’s acquisition of AccessOne, and displeasure accelerated with the stock plunging 26.6% after Q4 2026 earnings. Investors took no solace in the company’s major milestone in achieving full-year positive GAAP net income for the first time. Instead, downwardly revised revenue guidance and looming investments in AccessOne combined to undermine support for the stock. This vote of no confidence will take several quarters or more to repair. In the meantime, I have downgraded the stock from a buy to a hold.

Guidance

Last year, the company’s guidance was part of my bullish case for the stock. Now, the guidance has helped invalidate the bullish case. Phreesia lowered guidance for fiscal 2027 from a range of $545M to $559M to a range of $510M to $520M, a decline of 6.7% at the midpoint. Since the company has “shorter visibility into spending commitments by certain pharmaceutical manufacturers” and recently suffered “even lower levels of dollars committed by certain Network Solutions clients for the second half of the fiscal year,” I am not expecting upside surprises in the coming year. Network Solutions is Phreesia’s top-ranked grower in the business, followed by payments and then subscriptions.

Guidance for total revenue per Average Healthcare Services Client (AHSC) also fell, a key normalized metric for assessing Phreesia’s growth. The revenue headwinds forced the company to reduce revenue per AHSC guidance from a low double-digit range to a low single-digit percentage range.

Surprisingly, the company left intact its adjusted EBITDA guidance at a range of $125M to $135M for fiscal year 2027. Management explained that this guidance reflects “the operating leverage we have built and our ability to respond quickly with further efficiency gains.” Yet, this claim provided little reassurance.

An analyst even pushed back on the adjusted EBITDA guidance, given the company's reduced guidance in its high-margin business. Management responded with general references to explain how they will protect margins. For example, AI and outsourcing are replacing manual processes. The rest of the margin protection is predicated on a historic ability to support margins. Thus, specifics on margins will sit at the center of a “prove it” burden for Phreesia’s 2027 fiscal year.

The company’s claims about protecting margin also seem to run counter to needed investments in AccessOne: “We are actively working to expand our access to capital for our securitization programs so we can bring AccessOne solutions to a greater portion of our provider network.”

For now, AccessOne is “performing in line” with company expectations. Updates on progress will come in future quarters.

Overall, slowing company growth less than a year after its largest-ever acquisition raises red flags for me. Revenue grew 14.4% from 2025 to 2026. The midpoint of guidance suggests a halving in annual revenue growth to 7.2% and a turnaround that is out of sight for now.

Product Wins

Last year, I was greatly encouraged by a number of customer and product wins. The last quarter’s select reported wins are less encouraging given the business outlook. Still, the company recorded notable progress with VoiceAI.

Phreesia reported significant impact with VoiceAI, given $650K in cost savings in its first two months “primarily by eliminating dependence on expensive third-party call services and inefficiencies.” Team members used a 20,000-hour time savings to provide more direct patient support.

The company reported notable progress in its partnership with GoodRx Holdings, Inc. (GDRX). However, GoodRx did not mention this partnership in its Q4 earnings report, and the company expects “pressure on prescription transactions revenue in 2026.” Thus the 19M engagements, 1.6M requests for GoodRx coupon savings, and 476K claims using GoodRx coupons all seem less significant to Phreesia, even with the resulting $9.5M to $12M in patient savings. The long-term downtrend in GDRX also makes me wary.

Cash Flow

While PHR deteriorates in price, free cash flow margin continues to improve to new all-time highs.

Free cash flow equaled $28.5M, up from $19.3M a year ago and the company’s strongest ever quarterly free cash flow. Phreesia also reported that “year-over-year improvements in operating cash flow and free cash flow were driven primarily by changes in working capital and operating cash flows provided by AccessOne.”

Yet, cash and cash equivalents declined. As of January 31, 2026, cash dropped from $84.2M to $73.8M. Cash was boosted by proceeds from a $110M debt instrument in the quarter. The company spent $20M in principal payments on debt instruments.

A Tough Market

An unfavorable market environment also makes PHR a less attractive stock. Phreesia described this environment as “tough”: “The health care industry is facing adversity. We are seeing challenges in FDA guidelines, insurance coverage, patient utilization, and provider reimbursement.” While management noted that the company is growing despite the headwinds, the market looks like it will cap that growth at lower levels for the time being. Poor markets can exert continuous gravitational pull on the business.

Phreesia tried to reassure investors that the inability of life sciences customers to make longer-term spending commitments does not reflect poorly on the company’s competitive positioning or the demand for its products and services. The impact is “variability” in financial forecasting. However, the company’s results and market environment mean this variability reduces visibility for analysts and represents large downside risks relative to an eventual upside opportunity.

Moreover, spending and marketing on vaccines are down. Public health at the federal level used to be a growth area for the company. GLP-1s are also a source of slowdown in the Network Solutions business.

Thus, overall, Phreesia’s upside looks tightly capped for the foreseeable future.

Market Comparison

Phreesia’s peers are also struggling in 2026. While PHR is the worst-performing of the group with a -51% year-to-date performance (it was ranked 5 of 7 before earnings), the entire group is down significantly for the year. The best performer, HealthStream (HSTM), is down 11.3%.

This synchronized decline looks like a market that will stay tough for quite some time.

Stock-Based Compensation

The stock’s latest plunge makes stock-based compensation look ever more expensive for shareholders. The earnings report shows a fractionally small increase in stock-based compensation, but it represents a significant line item. The $67.5M in stock-based compensation for 2026 is the largest line item among operating activities. For further perspective, the company’s cost of revenue for the year was $71.4M. This 1.1 ratio of cost of revenue to stock-based compensation is significantly lower than peer Teladoc (TDOC). The company’s stock is trading just above its all-time low with a cost of revenue equal to $771.6M versus stock-based compensation of $80.4M, a 9.6 ratio (per the Q4 2025 earnings report). Teladoc slashed its stock-based compensation from $146.0M in the prior year.

Valuation and Rating

I previously claimed that double-digit revenue growth and improving free cash flow margin made PHR cheap on a valuation basis. The stock was trading at 3.4 times sales last September. Now, PHR is trading at a rock-bottom valuation of 1.0 times sales TTM (trailing twelve months) and forward. Thus, my downgrade of PHR to a hold symbolizes a stock in rating limbo. The stock is unattractive given the stalled growth, but it is “too late” to sell based on the valuation and all-time low in price. I will not consider upgrading the stock until the company returns to double-digit growth AND the stock manages to reverse its latest post-earnings losses.

Also note PHR’s quant rating flipped from a hold to a sell just ahead of Q4 earnings. Post-earnings, I interpret this rating as a “stay away” for new investors until further notice.

The Trade

PHR is now a “prove it” stock. Even though CEO Chaim Indig sounds hopeful by declaring “a more modest revenue growth year does not change our trajectory” because “it reflects a specific external dynamic in one part of our business,” the results, the guidance, and the market compel caution for me. The company has lost the market’s confidence, and it will need to significantly improve to begin turning things around.

Lingering in the background is also the ongoing “AI Panic,” usually called the SaaSpocalypse, which has compressed the valuations investors are willing to pay for software stocks. In future quarters, Phreesia will not just have the burden of demonstrating a quantifiable upside to its growth path, but also the company will need to make a concrete case for earning a higher valuation.

Phreesia is in a tough spot.

data.title: What Drives Returns In Floating Rate Notes?
data.description:
Originally Published on March 31, 2026

Returns in floating rate notes are driven by two main components: short-term interest rates and credit spreads.

Corporate floating rate notes (FRNs) are often used to help manage interest rate risk. Unlike fixed-rate bonds, FRNs are structured so that income adjusts with changes in short-term interest rates. As a result, their returns are driven primarily by income rather than price movements, making them potentially attractive when rates are rising or expected to remain elevated.

What Are Floating Rate Notes?

Corporate floating rate notes are bonds that pay a coupon linked to a short-term reference rate (usually SOFR), plus a fixed credit spread. The coupon resets periodically, allowing income to rise when short-term rates increase and decline when rates fall. Because coupons adjust regularly, FRN prices exhibit minimal sensitivity to changes in interest rates. Investors therefore avoid the duration-related price declines associated with traditional fixed-rate bonds, although they also do not benefit from falling rates through price appreciation.

Key Drivers of FRN Returns

Returns in corporate FRNs are driven by two main components: short-term interest rates and credit spreads.

Short-Term Interest Rates and Coupon Income

The primary source of FRN returns is coupons. As reference rates such as SOFR move in response to monetary policy, FRN coupons reset accordingly. When short-term rates rise, income increases; when rates fall, income declines. Given the low interest-rate duration of FRNs, price volatility from rate movements is minimal, leaving income as the dominant driver of performance.

Credit Spreads and Spread Duration

While interest rates drive the level of income, credit spreads determine how much additional yield investors earn for taking on issuer credit risk. Higher credit spreads generally result in higher income but also introduce sensitivity to changes in market credit conditions. This sensitivity, measured by spread duration, is distinct from interest-rate duration and reflects exposure to changes in credit conditions rather than policy rates. In general, a longer time to maturity is reflected in a higher spread duration.

How Interest Rate Environments Affect FRNs

FRNs in Rising Rate Environments

When short-term rates rise, corporate FRN coupons reset higher, increasing income. Because price sensitivity to rates is limited, rising yields do not create the same headwinds for FRNs as they do for fixed-rate bonds. Instead, higher income becomes the primary contributor to returns, as seen during recent “higher for longer” rate cycles.

FRNs When Rates Fall

The same dynamic works in reverse. During the period leading into the COVID-19 pandemic, rapid Federal Reserve rate cuts drove reference rates sharply lower, resulting in declining FRN coupons. While income fell, FRN prices remained relatively stable, reflecting their limited exposure to interest-rate-driven price volatility.

Short-Term Rates as the Primary Driver of FRN Income

Over time, corporate FRN returns reflect the combined effect of prevailing short-term rates and credit spreads. As policy rates and SOFR move, FRN coupons adjust accordingly, allowing investors to earn income that evolves with the rate environment while maintaining low interest-rate risk.

Why Floating Rate Notes May Help Manage Interest Rate Risk

Corporate FRNs offer a way to generate income while reducing sensitivity to rate changes. Although investors remain exposed to credit risk and spread volatility, FRNs allow returns to adjust with prevailing short-term rates rather than remaining locked into a fixed yield.

FRNs can serve as a low-duration income allocation for investors seeking yield with limited sensitivity to interest rate movements.

Important Disclosures

Index Descriptions:

MVIS® US Investment Grade Floating Rate Index (MVFLTR): iA modified market capitalization-weighted index that consists of U.S. dollar-denominated floating rate notes issued by corporate issuers and rated investment grade.

This is not an offer to buy or sell, or a recommendation to buy or sell any of the securities, financial instruments or digital assets mentioned herein. The information presented does not involve the rendering of personalized investment, financial, legal, tax advice, or any call to action. Certain statements contained herein may constitute projections, forecasts and other forward-looking statements, which do not reflect actual results, are for illustrative purposes only, are valid as of the date of this communication, and are subject to change without notice. Actual future performance of any assets or industries mentioned are unknown. Information provided by third party sources are believed to be reliable and have not been independently verified for accuracy or completeness and cannot be guaranteed. VanEck does not guarantee the accuracy of third party data. The information herein represents the opinion of the author(s), but not necessarily those of VanEck or its other employees.

An investor cannot invest directly in an index. Returns reflect past performance and do not guarantee future results. Results reflect the reinvestment of dividends and capital gains, if any. Certain indices may take into account withholding taxes. Index returns do not represent Fund returns. The Index does not charge management fees or brokerage expenses, nor does the Index lend securities, and no revenues from securities lending were added to the performance shown.

An investment in the Fund may be subject to risks which includes, among others, foreign securities, foreign currency, credit, interest rate, floating rate, restricted securities, financials sector, special risk considerations of investing in United Kingdom, European, and Australian issuers, market, operational, sampling, index tracking, authorized participant concentration, no guarantee of active trading market, trading issues, passive management, fund shares trading, premium/discount and liquidity of fund shares, non-diversified, and index-related concentration risks, all of which may adversely affect the Fund.

The VanEck IG Floating Rate ETF, which is based on the Floating Rate Index, is not issued, sponsored, endorsed, sold or marketed by ICE Data, and ICE Data makes no representation regarding the advisability of investing in such product.

Investing involves substantial risk and high volatility, including possible loss of principal. Bonds and bond funds will decrease in value as interest rates rise. An investor should consider the investment objective, risks, charges and expenses of the Fund carefully before investing. To obtain a prospectus and summary prospectus, which contains this and other information, call 800.826.2333 or visit vaneck.com. Please read the prospectus and summary prospectus carefully before investing.

© 2026 VanEck Securities Corporation, Distributor, a wholly owned subsidiary of Van Eck Associates Corporation.

Original Post

data.title: StoneCo: The Recent Drop Is A Golden Buying Opportunity (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Back when I last covered StoneCo (STNE), I highlighted their robust growth and strong financials, trading at an attractive valuation despite their significant potential to develop in Banking and Credit and even in the region over the longer term.

With the valuation getting more attractive following the recent price drop and the potential to see nearly 30% in shareholder returns in 2026 following the sale of their Linx business, STNE is upgraded to a Strong Buy.

Excellent Growth Continues

Despite a solid year with revenue growing by ~13%, STNE dropped significantly on the day of the release, recognizing a ~$30.6 million (BRL 157.8 million) loss from the Linx divestment and an expense of $9.4 million (BRL 48.5 million) for settlements related to civil lawsuits, while TPV growth was also below expectations, signaling a slowdown in merchant transactions - although the Adjusted EPS grew by 33.6% YoY.

StoneCo’s guidance for 2026 remained within their guidance, with a midpoint in Adjusted Basic EPS of $2.15 per share (BRL 11.1), while the 2027 level was cut to a midpoint of $2.44 (BRL 12.6), although the 2027 guidance assumes no impact from their potentially massive buybacks that can continue, while the one for 2026 doesn’t include the potential special distributions that I’ll mention below, which might be a significant understatement that opens the potential for a beat.

STNE also announced that the new managerial total capital ratio will drop from the previous safety cushion of at least 20% to 17%, which unlocks even more cash for the company to spend on growth or shareholder returns, being the result of their enhanced methodology, aiming to match the official standards used by the Brazilian central bank.

After running the company for ~3 years and executing several strategic initiatives (divesting non-core assets such as Linx, streamlining the business, introducing a capital allocation framework, and continuing the company's evolution), STNE's CEO announced his resignation back in early January, being replaced by the CFO, with the ex-CEO highlighting the following during their Q4 Earnings Call, potentially highlighting continuity in this direction:

I have complete confidence in Mateus' leadership. He has been one of the architects of Stone's transformation, helping restore discipline, simplify the business and focus the organization on what truly matters. He brings clarity of direction, analytical rigor and the right sense of urgency to accelerate execution and continue elevating what matters most, delivering value to our clients and building intrinsic value per share.

Financially, based on STNE’s latest report, they remain in a very strong position, with current assets covering their total liabilities and a manageable amount of debt despite increasing YoY. Again, this is distorted by their banking segment that’s growing significantly, but they continue to aim for an adjusted net cash position, with their adjusted net cash from continuing operations reaching $457 million (BRL 2.36 billion) in Q4.

Based on 2025's performance, the company expects to spend over $390 million (BRL 2 billion) on shareholder returns in the form of buybacks, which represents a buyback yield of >11.40% based on the current market cap of $3.42 billion, further showing the fact that the management - who also has plenty of exposure to the business as shareholders - continues to see the value in their stock.

Meanwhile, thanks to the Linx sale receiving the needed approvals, the company also expects to return an additional ~$600 million (BRL 3.08 billion) to shareholders, planning to define the distribution mechanism in April, meaning that we’re basically looking at a total yield of nearly 30% in 2026.

Rate Cut Hopes vs. Iran Uncertainty

One of the main, major developments that can boost STNE from a company and a stock point of view can be the potential Selic cuts expected this year, after the Brazilian Central Bank held rates at very elevated levels for the past few years, with the first of the long-awaited cuts being officially announced on March 18, although it was only 25 basis points (to 14.75% from 15.00%) instead of the hoped-for 50, as a result of the ongoing uncertainty in the Middle East, despite inflation reportedly cooling down before it.

This can be great for a company like STNE, as these cuts (with expectations now ranging from about 11.75% to 13.00% by the end of 2026) can potentially offer a boost in economic activity, as rate hikes slow down the economy to fight inflation, while cuts are theoretically doing the opposite. The company’s management also highlighted the following regarding it:

So basically, we're assuming Selic at low 12% by the end of 2026 and high 11% for 2027. That's what's behind the guidance number. On the credit side, basically, what you have is the result of a portfolio that keeps growing into different publics and different products, but also getting more mature, especially on the core product for the digital credit offerings that we have. So let me try to walk you through a bit of some of those moving parts.

Meanwhile, the USD gained as a result of a flight to safety due to the Iran conflict, but the BRL held relatively steady despite an initial jump, as the country can also win from the war premiums in their commodities, potentially highlighting their potential in the long term as a solid tool to hedge against this type of macro uncertainty - with Petrobras (PBR) nearly doubling YTD, making it one of the best performers in the oil and gas industry.

However, this conflict can have significant effects on these rate cut expectations. Although Brazil can win from this since the country is a major commodity producer, the potential inflationary shock as a result of a major jump in core raw materials/energy prices can also take its toll on the Central Bank of Brazil’s decisions surrounding interest rates.

This was highlighted by the Fed during Powell’s latest speech, where he announced that the Fed wouldn’t cut and warned about the potential inflationary shock, while the ECB chief said they’re ready to hike rates as soon as possible while warning about the potentially underestimated effects that Iran’s retaliatory attacks had on the region’s infrastructure, potentially leading to months or even more until flows would come back to normal.

Inflation (following the pandemic and the subsequent money printing) was the main reason why we had these rate hikes in the first place, and there’s certainly the possibility to see them pressure Brazil as well for longer. However, I’ll also highlight that there’s a lot of room for improvements in the economy since the country can get back on the long-term trend of rate reductions, potentially offering a long-term fundamental boost to its economic activity while also pushing money away from government bonds into equity, with STNE being a solid candidate for high growth that’s also reflecting the economy it depends on.

Risks Reiterated

Ultimately, STNE will depend on the environment by default, while the ongoing uncertainty and potential pressure caused by the Iran conflict can add a lot of volatility and pressure on their valuation.

Meanwhile, competition remains a very significant issue to keep in mind, as STNE is far from the only player that’s there to capitalize on the region’s digitalization growth potential, which can pressure margins and potential to develop in the long term, even though I continue to consider Brazil the pillar of their future expansion in the region, which in itself comes with specific risks.

Valuation

For STNE’s DCF to Equity, to get to the FCFE equivalent, since we’re talking about a financial business, I’ll adjust their Adj. Net Income of $480 million is seen in 2025 with a retention rate (i.e., the CAGR divided by the ROE) of 40% throughout the entire period to account for the money that basically funds their growth, getting to an FCFE of $288 million in 2025.

Based on the current 248.9 million number of shares, that’s an FCFE/share of $1.157 in 2025, and given the implied CAGR they estimate in their EPS from 2025 to 2027, I'll use 20% as a conservative estimate to account for the potential for buybacks to boost this beyond their expected ~13.92% based on the guidance, followed by a 12% CAGR until 2030 and 8% until 2035, despite the potential for an international expansion that can continue keeping this in the double-digit range (especially if combined with massive buybacks), with a 2.5% terminal growth rate and a 12.5% discount rate to take into account the competitive and general macro risks for the region.

This way, we get to an equity value of $5.74 billion, for an intrinsic value of $23.08 per share, which remains well above the current $13.95 at the moment of writing this, and that’s when using relatively conservative estimates.

Conclusions

STNE is upgraded to Strong Buy, with the valuation getting to a level that’s very hard to ignore while they stand to benefit significantly from potential macro improvements and an expansion, despite recent pressure.

There are risks to keep in mind given the industry and the country, but the potential for a long-term international pivot, massive buybacks that can continue since they’re funded by their cash flow, on top of the region’s digitalization trends.

data.title: Capturing Consistent Return Streams In Capricious Equity Markets
data.description:
Originally Published on March 26, 2026

By Joshua Lisser

How can an active core equity portfolio deliver more reliable long-term results from a modest alpha target?

For much of the past decade, equity investing has been defined by abundance. Strong beta, sustained momentum and a narrow group of market leaders meant that returns often came easily, just by being invested. When markets delivered double-digit gains, consistency was largely taken for granted.

That backdrop is changing. Return expectations are lower, market leadership is more concentrated, and factor and style rotations have become more volatile. For many active managers, beating benchmarks consistently has become more difficult—and taking large, high-conviction positions increasingly risky.

So is there a way to reap the benefits of active management without assuming undue risks? Instead of searching for the next outsize opportunity, we think investors should look for portfolios designed to deliver repeatable results across different market regimes. In a lower-return world, even modest excess returns can meaningfully improve long-term outcomes, and consistency will become an increasingly valuable asset in its own right.

Style Volatility Raises Performance Hurdles

Consistently beating a benchmark is harder than ever. In recent years, the MSCI World Index outperformed 67% of actively managed funds in the eVestment Global Blend Universe (Display). To some extent, this trend was driven by the dominance of the US mega stocks, which complicated efforts by active equity managers to deviate from the benchmark without taking on too much risk.

Active Managers Have Struggled Amid Increased Style Volatility

Style volatility has also raised hurdles to performance. Although we strongly believe traditional style-based investing, such as growth and value equities, has historically offered long-term return benefits, investors must often cope with long periods of underperformance along the way. Over the last 10 years, the dispersion of factor index returns widened significantly compared with 2005–2015, meaning investors paid a heavy penalty for choosing the wrong style at the wrong time.

Combining Fundamental Insights and Quantitative Controls

So how can investors overcome these challenges? As we see it, the key to consistency is to create an active portfolio that combines the benefits of broad fundamental research and quantitative tools.

This approach is based on a simple philosophy. Skilled fundamental analysts are great at identifying undervalued securities that can outperform over the long term. Quantitative risk controls can help manage portfolio risk and neutralize factor tilts, which supports more consistent results. In other words, this type of strategy can isolate high-conviction active positions while deploying quantitative tools to optimize a portfolio and ultimately overcome the erratic style winds that may blow a portfolio off course.

Fundamental Research: Company Expertise Still Matters

Style risk feels particularly acute today. The rapid growth of artificial intelligence has driven both the outsized returns of hyperscalers and adjacent names and has punished companies perceived to be at risk of disruption, notably those in enterprise software. Heightened geopolitical stresses have also added to this cocktail of risk. We think deep company research can pave the way to differentiated positions in broader parts of the market. But sharpening stockpicking in a core equity portfolio requires access to a large pool of fundamental research across the style spectrum.

Skilled value, growth and core equity managers have expertise in different investing disciplines. Value investors search for underappreciated shares of companies with quality businesses and improvement prospects by focusing on the disconnect between fundamentals and valuation. Growth portfolios target accelerating and profitable growth, while core equity investors look for steady compounders derived from resilient long-term cash flows. Tapping into fundamental research across styles enables an equity strategy to choose the highest-conviction holdings with complementary features that can provide balance through changing factor regimes.

Quantitative Building Blocks for Portfolio Construction

But sourcing an array of fundamentally attractive holdings doesn’t provide adequate risk control on its own. Quantitative research can make the difference by addressing critical questions.

What are the expected returns for all portfolio candidates and holdings? How do securities sourced across the market diversify one another? How can we limit unintended or uncompensated risks in the portfolio? Does the strategy’s risk budget warrant constraints on specific position sizes?

Quantitative models can answer these questions and apply them to portfolio construction. Well-designed quantitative tools can help limit factor exposures and augment returns from security selection while calibrating weights to broaden risk. The model can be designed to optimize holdings and create a well-structured equity portfolio.

A Sturdier Foundation for Smoother Return Patterns

This framework supports steadier investment outcomes in three ways.

First, it can construct a portfolio with index-like characteristics. That means there are no big sector, country, or factor tilts, which often fuel volatility.

Second, limiting a portfolio’s vulnerability to those biases allows stock selection to be the primary return driver.

Third, taken together, this structure supports a relatively modest excess return target at a similarly modest tracking error. Instead of shooting for the stars, we think this type of strategy can beat the benchmark without suffering wild swings that may undermine confidence in turbulent markets. In fact, our research suggests that core equity portfolios with lower tracking error have outperformed over time (Display).

Low Relative Risk Hits the Sweet Spot in Core Equity Portfolios

Where Does the Strategy Fit?

These benefits create a portfolio with a distinctive place in an equity allocation that can complement passive or active portfolios for distinct investing needs.

While passive portfolios have become popular in recent years, many investors are concerned about market concentration risks and more modest returns moving forward. If US mega-cap dominance wanes and market returns broaden, passive portfolios with heavy weights in the technology titans could suffer. In a lower equity market return environment, the opportunity to generate alpha may become significantly more important than it has been in years past. Passive investors can prepare for a scenario like this by adding a strategy with modest alpha potential.

Concentration has also created a conundrum for investors in traditional active equity strategies, many of whom have suffered disappointing relative returns. Investors who believe in the power of active stockpicking but want to reduce the risk of underperformance can swap out some of an active allocation toward a strategy that aims to generate consistent outperformance while reducing risk.

Generating reliable return patterns is becoming increasingly valuable. When executed with discipline, this type of equity portfolio can offer the best of both worlds: passive-like risk characteristics with a moderate but significant dollop of alpha that compounds nicely over time. In today’s markets, that type of consistency isn’t a consolation prize—it’s a foundation for better long‑term outcomes.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams and are subject to change over time.

MSCI makes no express or implied warranties or representations, and shall have no liability whatsoever with respect to any MSCI data contained herein. The MSCI data may not be further redistributed or used as a basis for other indices or any securities or financial products. This report is not approved, reviewed or produced by MSCI.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
